coredata.dc:description: The development of functional materials with uniquely advanced properties lies at the core of nanoscience and nanotechnology. From the myriad possible combinations of organic and/or inorganic blocks, hybrids combining metal nanoclusters and carbon nanomaterials have emerged as highly attractive colloidal materials for imaging, sensing (optical and electrochemical) and catalysis, among other applications. While the metal nanoclusters provide extraordinary luminescent and electronic properties, the carbon nanomaterials (of zero, one or two dimensions) convey versatility, as well as unique interfacial, electronic, thermal, optical, and mechanical properties, which altogether can be put to use for the desired application. Herein, we present an overview of the field, for experts and non-experts, encompassing the basic properties of the building blocks, a systematic view of the chemical preparation routes and physicochemical properties of the hybrids, and a critical analysis of their ongoing and emerging applications. Challenges and opportunities, including directions towards green chemistry approaches, are also discussed.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (I) Schematics of the production of a hybrid between GSH-AuNCs and Cdots-TiO2 through the EDAC/NHS coupling reaction, (II) TEM imaging of (a) Cdots, (b) Cdots-TiO2 hollow microspheres, (c) GSH-AuNCs and (d) GSH-AuNCs/Cdots-TiO2 hybrids, and (III) fluorescence emission spectra of the respective systems shown in (a-d). Adapted from [22].

(I) Schematics for the formation of AuNCs onto CNTs using CeO2 NPs as anchoring sites. (II) (a) TEM and (b) HRTEM imaging of AuNCs onto CeO2/PC-MWCNTs (microwave method); and (III) (a) TEM and (b) HRTEM imaging of AuNCs onto CeO2/PC-MWCNTs (NH3 method). Insets show detailed areas of TEM images shown in both (a). Adapted from [159].

(I) Top and side views of 2D carbon nanosheets studied by DFT/LDA for non-decorated (top) and Au decorated (bottom) surfaces. (II) TEM images of Au coatings onto MWCNTs (a) without further treatment and (b) oxygen-plasma-treated to form oxygenated vacancies. Adapted from [18].

(I) Schematics of the preparation of a FA-GO/Lys-AuNCs hybrid (top) and of the detection of cancer cell through the interaction between hybrids and folate receptors. (II) (a), (b) TEM imagens of FA-GO/Lys-AuNCs under different magnifications. Adapted from [171].

(I) Schematics of the in situ production of DNA-AgNCs/N-GO onto GCE and (II) respective (a) TEM and (b) HRTEM imaging of DNA-AgNCs/N-GO/GCE, with the inset showing the lattice spacing of AgNCs. (II)(c) TEM image of DNA-AgNCs/N-GO/GCE after long-term operation. Inset shows the unchanged lattice spacing after long-term operation. Adapted from [59].

(I) Schematics of fabrication of Tf-AuNCs/GO hybrids for turn-off-and-on NIR fluorescent bioimaging of cancer cells. (II) Fluorescence spectra of Tf-AuNCs under excitation at 380 nm. Inset shows Tf-AuNCs dispersion under (a) ambient light and (b) UV irradiation. (III) Fluorescence imaging of HeLa tumor-bearing mice after 480 min of intratumorally injection of Tf-AuNCs/GO fluorescent probe. Adapted from [21].

(I) Schematics of the assembly of a visual dual-emitting sensor for the detection of water based on Cdots and CuNCs. (II) PL spectra and (III) photographs under UV light of CuNCs/Cdots dual-emitting film in different humidity environments, ranging from 11% to 94% of water at 25°C. Adapted from [118].

(I) Schematics of assembly of BSA-AuNCs/Cdots dual emission fluorescence probe and its selective detection of metal cations, Hg2+ and Cr6+. (II) Fluorescence emission spectra of bare BSA-AuNCs (red) and Cdots (blue), and their hybrid AuNCs/Cdots (pink). (III) Selective fluorescence response of AuNCs/Cdots towards Hg2+ and Cr6+ separately and a mixture of both ions. The inset shows an enlarged area of the spectra corresponding to red emission, for detailed analysis. Taken from [113]. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(I) Schematics of the production of AuNCs onto rGO sheets for ORR and (II) (a) SEM and (b) TEM images of the AuNCs/rGO hybrid. (III) Rotating disk electrode curves of commercial Pt/C, Au/rGO hybrids, AuNP/rGO hybrids, rGO sheets, and AuNCs in O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH at a scanning rate of 50 mV/s at 1600 rpm. Adapted from [20].

Reduction of 4-NP catalyzed by hybrids of MNCs and 2D CNMs. (I) (a) Schematics of the mechanism; (b) Absorption of UV–Vis light by GSH-AuNCs/rGO on the catalytic reduction of 4-NP to 4-AP. The inset shows the color change from yellow to colorless after the reaction occurred. Taken from [198]. (II) (a) Formation of the reactive Au–H species onto the surface of AuNCs catalyst by reaction with NaBH4; (b) catalytic mechanism of the reduction of 4-NP in dark; (c) catalytic mechanism of 4-NP reduction under Visible light irradiation with the possible generation of H2 by the reduction of H2O. Adapted from [176]. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Schematics and TEM images of (a) paramagnetic Ho 3+-complexed GO nanosheets and (b) AuNCs forming a (c) Ho-GO-Au hybrid with antibacterial properties. (II) Schematics and fluorescence imaging of S. aureus after 2 h treatment with GO-Au, Ho-GO under magnetic prealignment (Ho-GO(+)), and Ho-GO-Au under magnetic prealignment (Ho-GO-Au(+)), in comparison with the bacteria in aqueous environment (top line). Both merged and enlarged images show dead cells in red and total cells in blue. Scale bars are 25 μm. Adapted from [195]. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Energy diagram and optical properties of metals in the form of free atoms, small nanoclusters, and larger nanoparticles. Size-dependent fluorescence emission and surface plasmon resonance behavior are the key features of MNCs and MNPs, respectively.
Overview of the most common bottom-up approach for the synthesis of ligand-protected MNCs (to further form hybrids with CNMs), including metal ion sources, reducing agents, and protecting ligands.
Carbon nanomaterials of various dimensions and their main properties, relevant for the formation of superior hybrid nanomaterials.
Examples of carbon sources used in the bottom-up hydrothermal synthesis of Cdots (to further prepare hybrids with MNCs).
The most common synthetic pathways to obtain hybrids of MNCs and CNMs (with the percentages estimated based on the relative number of publications).

Coupling reaction between MNCs and Cdots using the EDAC/NHS protocol. The carboxylic groups in the ligands of MNCs (gray halo) react with EDAC and sulfo-NHS to form a reactive intermediate product. Then, amino groups in the surface of Cdots react with the intermediate to obtain the MNC/CNM hybrid by covalent bonding. Coupling reaction can also occur starting from carboxylic groups in the surface of Cdots to couple with amino groups in the ligands of MNCs.

Sensing mechanisms of MNC/CNM hybrids. (a) Ratiometric dual-emitting MNC/Cdots nanoprobes with an internal reference signal. The photoluminescence (PL) emissions are independent from each other and, upon addition of an analyte the MNCs fluorescence is quenched, while that of the Cdots remains unchanged. (b) Ratiometric dual-emitting fluorescence nanoprobe based on FRET between Cdots and MNCs. FRET occurs when the distance between donor and acceptor is <10 nm and the emission of the donor overlaps with the absorption of the acceptor. PL emission of Cdots quenches due to FRET, and, upon addition of an analyte that inhibits the FRET process, the MNCs fluorescence decreases, while that of Cdots recovers gradually. (c) Turn-off-and-on sensing mechanisms are common for hybrids made of MNCs and 1D or 2D CNMS. The interaction between MNCs and CNMs quenches the PL emission of the MNCs (turn-off), which is recovered upon addition of an analyte (turn-on).
Main catalytic applications of hybrids composed of MNCs and CNMs of different dimensions.
Summary of biomacromolecules-sensing methods based on hybrids of MNCs and carbon nanomaterials of various dimensionalities (0D, 1D and 2D).
Summary of sensing methods of small molecules based on fluorescence emission of MNC/CNM hybrids.
Summary of ion-sensing methods based on MNC/CNM hybrids.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of functional materials with uniquely advanced properties lies at the core of nanoscience and nanotechnology. From the myriad possible combinations of organic and/or inorganic blocks, hybrids combining metal nanoclusters and carbon nanomaterials have emerged as highly attractive colloidal materials for imaging, sensing (optical and electrochemical) and catalysis, among other applications. While the metal nanoclusters provide extraordinary luminescent and electronic properties, the carbon nanomaterials (of zero, one or two dimensions) convey versatility, as well as unique interfacial, electronic, thermal, optical, and mechanical properties, which altogether can be put to use for the desired application. Herein, we present an overview of the field, for experts and non-experts, encompassing the basic properties of the building blocks, a systematic view of the chemical preparation routes and physicochemical properties of the hybrids, and a critical analysis of their ongoing and emerging applications. Challenges and opportunities, including directions towards green chemistry approaches, are also discussed.

ce:keyword.ce:text: inductively coupled plasma - atomic emission spectrometry

ce:section.ce:para: Hybrid nanomaterials stand out as a fascinating and promising research field within the realm of nanoscience and nanotechnology. These intriguing materials can be defined as mixtures of two or more inorganic and/or organic components, with interpenetration of phases on a sub-micrometric (typically nanometric) scale [1]. The combination of the colloid-sized building blocks into hybrids usually gives rise to novel or synergistic effects and properties, ultimately leading to the creation of multifunctional and unique materials. Since the combinatorial possibilities are virtually endless, the challenges in forming the hybrids are closely related to the production of the building blocks themselves, and to the discovery of the ways to fabricate suitable mixtures of them [2]. Proper design and preparation of this type of hierarchical materials lead to tunable properties for advanced applications, such as sensing and imaging [3–5], chemical catalysis [6], biomimetic nanocatalysis [7], solar cells [8], fuel cells [9], electronics [10,11] and drug delivery [12], among others.

Amidst the profusion of hybrid colloidal materials investigated, those composed of metal nanoclusters (MNCs) and carbon nanomaterials (CNMs) of various dimensions are particularly interesting and challenging. They offer a panoply of potential applications, depending on the nature of the building blocks and on their interaction. CNMs may not only improve the stability of the MNCs against aggregation but also provide enhanced interfacial (e.g. electrochemical) performance due to their large specific surface area and high electrical conductivity [13]. However, since the electrical properties of CNMs can vary from metallic to semiconducting and their surface chemistry is also widely diverse [14,15], the exact nature of the nanocarbon is critical for the type of interaction with the clusters, and ultimately for the physicochemical properties and applications of the hybrid itself.

MNC/CNM hybrids have been increasingly studied in the past 15–20 years, starting mainly in the early 2000s with the combination of nanoclusters of either copper [16], platinum [17] or gold [18] on one side, and carbon nanotubes (CNTs) on the other side. Since the early 2010s, there has been a growing interest in hybrids of gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) with graphene-based 2D nanomaterials [19–21] and, later on, with carbon dots (Cdots) [22–24]. Despite this increasing attention towards MNC/CNM combinations, only a few reviews have covered hybrids of MNCs with different types of materials [25], including carbon quantum dots [26] and graphene-based materials [27,28]. There is lack of a comprehensive analysis, offering a broad and integrated overview on the preparation of such materials, their properties and potentialities, main challenges, and future perspectives. Herein, we aim at filling this gap, addressing the most significant contributions in this research area, ranging from the synthesis of the hybrids to their main properties and applications.

This review is organized as follows. First, we address the most relevant properties of the building blocks, MNCs and CNMs, making the bridge to the fabrication of hybrids. Afterwards, we cover and systematize the main synthetic pathways to produce MNC/CNM hybrids and discuss the main properties obtained. Then, we focus on the most important applications of these materials, namely on sensing, imaging, and catalysis, with some interesting studies also appearing in theragnostics and antimicrobial activity. To conclude, we offer a perspective on the future of this type of advanced nanomaterials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Metal nanoclusters (MNCs): ultra-small colloids with unique properties
ce:section.ce:para: Dispersions containing particles with at least one linear dimension in the approximate range of 1 nm to 1 μm constitute an important type of colloidal systems. Among the wide diversity of colloidal particles that can be dispersed, metal nanoparticles with atomically precise size and shape are of great interest, especially because of their applications in optics, sensing, imaging, catalysis, and nanomedicine. Despite that gold nanoparticles have been studied for a long time (ever since the pioneering work of Faraday in 1857), it was only in the last two decades or so that metal nanoparticles in general (of controlled size and shape) were investigated at a deeper level [29]. Metal nanoclusters, a particular case of metal particles, consist of ultra-small assemblies of metal atoms with up to 2 nm, a size so small that they do not exhibit surface plasmon behavior. Lacking plasmonic properties, MNCs are closer to molecules than to metal nanoparticles. In fact, MNCs present discrete electronic energy levels and size-dependent fluorescence emission, with high photostability [30,31]. Quantum confinement effects arise from the cluster dimensions, comparable to the Fermi's wavelength of the metal. The photoluminescence emission varies from blue, for smaller MNCs, to red, as they grow closer to 2 nm. Scheme 1 shows the energy levels and main optical properties of metal particles of various sizes. For MNCs, the energy levels and optical properties are strictly related with the type of metal, the size of the clusters, and the stabilizing ligand surrounding the metal core, and therefore the actual energy levels are more complex and difficult to predict [31].

Most of the work on MNCs has dealt with clusters of noble metals (Au, Ag, Pt and Pd) and Cu, or some alloyed form containing those metals. While small MNCs (3–9 atoms) are stable at small concentration on their bare, unprotected form, larger ones (> 10 atoms) are often stabilized by protecting ligands, like bovine serum albumin (BSA), poly(amidoamine) dendrimer or thiolated-ligands. Notably, there is a series of “magic” numbers of free electrons (2, 8, 18, 20, 34, 58, …) that results in clusters with high stability [30].

Since the properties of MNCs are highly size-dependent, the synthesis of size-controlled, stable clusters is a crucial step in their preparation [32]. Synthetic methods can be broadly divided in top-down and bottom-up: in the former, the nanocluster is built based on the etching of metal nanoparticles, while the latter entails atom-by-atom building, starting from metal ion precursors. MNCs aimed at coupling with CNMs are typically prepared through bottom-up methods, whereby a metal ion precursor is reduced to metal zero-valent atoms in a nucleation process. Restricting the nuclei growth, usually by adding a stabilizing agent, results in sized-controlled MNCs [33]. Scheme 2 summarizes the typical bottom-up route leading to ligand-protected MNCs. Recent reports further describe the most used pathways to synthesize MNCs and their as-obtained characteristics [34–42].

In this review, we focus on clusters of gold, silver and copper, which represent over 85% of the published data so far on MNCs assembled with CNMs. Gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) are by far the most studied (> 50% of the published data), mainly because of their remarkable optical and catalytic properties, as well as their comparatively lower reactivity [43–54]. Silver nanoclusters (AgNCs) have also attracted great attention, mainly because of their optical properties (viz. excellent photostability and strong luminescence), high biocompatibility and antimicrobial activity [55–62]. Copper nanoclusters (CuNCs) are the most investigated non-noble metal NCs, as they have similar optical properties to noble metal NCs and are equally excellent catalysts, but at much lower cost [63,64]. Clusters of other noble metal beyond Au and Ag have also been approached in recent years, namely platinum (PtNCs) [17,65–67] and palladium (PdNCs) [68,69] ones, owing to their excellent catalytic properties. Although there is growing interest on new types of MNCs and bimetallic clusters [70], nanoclusters of Au, Ag and Cu are still significantly more investigated than all others.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbon nanomaterials (CNMs) as building blocks of hybrids
ce:section.ce:para: New allotropic forms of carbon, ranging from 0D, like carbon quantum dots (CQDs), to 1D to 2D, like CNTs and graphene (G), respectively, have been at the forefront of nanoscience and nanotechnology over the last 15–20 years, in view of their unique electronic, mechanical, thermal, and optical properties, often combined in a single material. Beyond their intrinsic properties, CNMs have also emerged as building blocks to construct nanohybrids or nanocomposites with special or advanced properties. Carbon-based nanomaterials, in particular, have come forth as good candidates for hybrids with nanoclusters, as the carbon matrices are able to reduce metal ions and stabilize them, aiding the production of atomically precise MNCs. Furthermore, the broad variety of CNMs give rise to hybrids with distinct properties and potential applications. Scheme 3 provides a summary of the most outstanding properties of CNMs, with respect to their dimensionality. A concise overview of the main characteristics of carbon dots, carbon nanotubes and graphene-based sheets is also provided below.

0D: carbon-based quantum dots. Herein, the term Cdots is used to encompass single-layer graphene quantum dots (GQDs), multi-layer quasi-spherical crystalline carbon quantum dots (CQDs), and amorphous carbon nanodots (CNDs), the latter sometimes also named as polymer dots. This designation, Cdots, overcomes a certain inconsistency in terminology across the literature, and the often lack of clear-cut characterization of the fine structure of the dots. Cdots are 0D carbon nanoparticles, either crystalline or amorphous, with sizes typically ranging between 1 and 10 nm (up to, at most, few tens of nanometers) and, like MNCs, they also present size-, excitation- and pH-dependent emission. The mechanism behind the luminescence of Cdots is rather complex and relies mainly on quantum confinement effects, small domains of sp2 carbon embedded in the carbonaceous core, surface defects and energy traps [71,72]. They behave as semiconductor quantum dots, but with the main advantage of having low toxicity, an important feature for biological and medical applications [72–76].

The synthesis, photophysical properties and applications in sensing, imaging, catalysis, and energy-related devices (photovoltaics, OLEDs, supercapacitors, and batteries) of Cdots have been extensively reviewed [77–88]. Similarly to MNCs, Cdots are colloidal particles with two main routes of production: bottom-up or top-down. The bottom-up method typically involves the solvothermal (hydrothermal, if the solvent is water) treatment of a carbon source (such as polymers, proteins or small molecules), as shown in Scheme 4 . The top-down method starts with a higher-dimension carbon matrix, like graphene sheets, and delivers the Cdots through exfoliation or etching [89]. In this review, unless otherwise stated, all types of Cdots have been prepared by the bottom-up method, the most common one, using different carbon sources. Another key aspect is the functionalization of Cdots with O- and N-containing groups, a very useful method to tune their optical properties and form hybrids via electrostatic interactions or covalent bonding [90]. Surface functional groups (e.g. OH, COOH and NH2) also enhance the water-solubility and biocompatibility of Cdots, two critical issues for biomedical applications [91].

1D: carbon nanotubes. CNTs are one dimensional carbon nanomaterials with a unique molecular structure. Single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) can be envisaged as single rolled-up graphene sheets of diameters between 0.8 and 2 nm, whereas multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) are composed of multiple sheets nested inside one-another and bound together by van der Waals forces, with diameters of 5 to 20 nm [92]. Each carbon atom in the graphene sheet is connected to three other carbons by σ bonds, in a sp2 hybridized hexagonal network. The electrons in the non-hybridized p orbitals of adjacent carbon atoms create a conjugated π system, making CNTs electrically conductive (metallic or semi-conducting, depending on chirality and curvature). The remarkable mechanical, optical, thermal, and electrical properties of CNTs, together with their extremely high aspect and surface-to-volume ratio, have been explored in a wide range of applications from field effect transistors, supercapacitors and batteries, to drug delivery and sensing [93].

CNTs are mostly produced through large scale chemical vapor deposition (CVD). Pristine CNTs are commercially available in large amounts and hence, unlike Cdots, typically they are not produced in laboratory [92]. The high aspect ratio and strong van der Waals cohesive forces between CNTs result in their tight agglomeration into bundles, ropes or entangled networks, a drawback for their applicability, which often requires individualized nanotubes. Colloidal dispersions of well-separated CNTs usually require the use of organic solvents, or surface functionalization of the carbon surface by covalent on non-covalent methods (both especially relevant for aqueous solvents), in all cases coupled to strong shear mixing, like ultrasonication [94]. Covalent functionalization relies on chemical reactions to add functional groups (e.g. OH, COOH and NH2) to the carbon surface [94]. Non-covalent functionalization requires the physical adsorption of amphiphiles (namely surfactants [95–97] and polymers [98,99], including proteins [97,100]) onto the nanotube surface, which changes its interfacial properties, such as hydrophilicity, wettability or surface charge, leading to well-dispersed CNTs with high kinetic colloidal stability. Hybrids containing CNTs also require in most cases previous nanotube surface functionalization and immobilization, which represents both a challenge and an opportunity. For example, non-covalent functionalization, involving either hydrophobic, electrostatic or π-π interactions (or combinations thereof) was found key to build advanced hybrids for electrochemical sensing [101,102] and the electrocatalysis of energy reactions relevant to fuel cells [103,104].

2D: graphene-based materials. Graphene, graphene oxide (GO), and graphitic carbon nitride nanosheets (g-C3N4) are the most explored 2D nanomaterials, either in pristine or functionalized form, in hybrids with MNCs [105]. Graphene is the designation given to a single layer of a hexagonal network of sp2 hybridized carbon atoms, which displays a remarkable combination of properties [106]. In addition to high flexibility and high specific surface area, it shows very large mechanical strength and conducts heat and electricity extremely well (viz. ballistic charge transport) along its plane. It is a strong absorber of all spectral range of visible light, which accounts for its black color, yet a single sheet is nearly transparent due to its minute thickness [106]. In as-produced graphene nanosheets, the actual number of layers is a critical feature, as the electronic structure rapidly changes with this number (the properties approach the 3D limit of graphite for ≥10 layers). Graphene's properties have been explored in many applications, like electronics, sensing, photonics, photovoltaics, energy storage and composites [106–108]. The oxidized graphene, GO, obtained mainly by the Hummers' method from graphite powder, is easily functionalized, and has been directly used for biomedical applications, such as imaging and drug delivery [106–108].

Graphene is nowadays mostly produced by CVD, although exfoliation of graphite was the major technique used for many years [10,109,110]. Like CNTs, graphene can be functionalized by covalent or non-covalent methods [111]. The graphene analogue, g-C3N4, is usually obtained through liquid-phase exfoliation of the bulk material, in turn prepared by pyrolysis of a nitrogen-rich precursor, such as melamine, thiourea, urea or cyanamide [112]. g-C3N4 has been applied in photocatalysis, sensing, and energy storage, owing mainly to its electron-rich properties, tunable and relatively narrow band gap, and facile biodegradability [112].
In the following section, the main synthetic pathways and as-obtained properties of MNC/CNM hybrids, based on published works, are addressed in detail.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of MNC/CNM nanohybrids

ce:section.ce:para: MNC/Cdot hybrids exhibit highly interesting dual-emission properties. They have been investigated for fluorescence imaging and noninvasive monitoring of different types of biologically relevant analytes (e.g. reactive oxygen species, small molecules, proteins, and ions) in living cells and small animal models.

Non-covalent interactions. The most widespread method involves mixing dispersions of oppositely charged MNCs and Cdots, taking advantage of the electrostatic interactions between the two materials [23,42,113–120]. Barman et al. prepared hybrids of positively charged polyethyleneimine-capped Cdots (PEI-Cdots) with an average diameter (ø) of 4–5 nm, and negatively charged BSA-capped AuNCs (BSA-AuNCs) of ø = 2 ± 1 nm [23]. Similarly, Wang et al. prepared a hybrid using negatively charged glutathione-capped AuNCs (GSH-AuNCs) and positively charged amino-functionalized Cdots (N-Cdots) [115]. Typically, these AuNCs/Cdots hybrids showed dual luminescence bands, with blue emission coming from Cdots and red emission from MNCs [116]. Furthermore, Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) between Cdots and AuNCs was reported when the separation between them in the hybrid was <10 nm [113,114,117,120,121].

Another approach involves the formation of hydrogen bonds between Cdots and MNCs, leading to self-assembled hybrids [24,60,113,122–126]. In this case, the dots and clusters can be both positively [113,123] or both negatively [60,125,126] charged, but must contain appropriate functional groups to make bridging H-bonds between the two types of particles. For instance, BSA-AgNCs and N-doped Cdots, both exhibiting negative zeta potentials (ζ = −4.66 mV for BSA-AgNCs and ζ = −22.40 mV for N-Cdots) formed hybrids via van der Waals interactions and hydrogen bonding [60]. A similar approach was taken by Yue et al., who developed hybrids based on both negatively charged BSA-AgNCs (ζ = −28.5 mV) and N,Fe-doped Cdots (ζ = −9.54 mV, due to partial ionization of the surface hydroxyl and carboxyl groups) [127]. The UV–Vis absorption of BSA-AgNCs overlapped with the fluorescence of N, Fe-Cdots, and after the hybrid was formed, the fluorescence of the nanoclusters was quenched due to the inner filter effect (IFE).

Covalent bonding. Many groups have used a coupling chemical reaction strategy to form hybrids between AuNCs and Cdots [47,128–136]. For instance, Ju et al. separately prepared AuNCs (ø ~ 2 nm) and N, S-doped Cdots (ø ~ 7 nm), then obtained a nanohybrid upon activation of the surface carboxylic groups of GSH-AuNCs by EDAC and further addition of sulfo-NHS to promote crosslinking with primary amines on the Cdots surface (Scheme 6). The hybrid particles were about 50 nm in size, with multiple AuNCs and Cdots in a single particle. Likewise, DHLA-AuNCs, [47] BSA-AuNCs [131,133,134], MUA-AuNCs [129], GSH-AuNCs [130,135], and CuNCs [132], all with ø ~ 2 nm, were covalently bonded to Cdots (ø = 2–5 nm) from various carbon sources using the carbodiimide coupling reaction. In all cases, the nanohybrid showed dual emission at 440–450 nm (Cdots) and 620–640 nm (MNCs), with the dots emission overlapping the clusters absorption. In this scenario, and since the two particles were in close vicinity (< 10 nm) due to the covalent bonding, FRET occurred between the Cdots (donors) and the MNCs (acceptors).

Yang et al. used the same molecule, papain, a cysteine protease enzyme derived from papaya, to form Cdots (ø ~ 5.3 nm) and protect AuNCs (ø ~ 2.0 nm) [137]. They prepared a hybrid using the EDAC/NHS protocol with two emission bands centered at 450 nm and 632 nm, upon excitation at 370 nm. Alternatively, Wang et al. used the same coupling reaction to prepare AuNCs/Cdots hybrids inside hollow spheres of TiO2 (Fig. 1 ) [22]. The Cdots-doped TiO2 microspheres were surface-functionalized with NH2 groups by reaction with (3-aminopropyl)trimethoxysilane (APTMS) in a chloroform/acetone mixture. Subsequently, a carbodiimide-activated coupling reaction was performed between GSH-AuNCs and the functionalized Cdots-doped TiO2 microspheres. The final hybrid was a hollow dual-emitting microsphere with blue-emitting Cdots (ø ~ 2.5 nm) inside the TiO2 spheres, and red-emitting AuNCs (ø ~ 2.1 nm) decorating the outer surface. These core-shell particles had an average ø ~ 207 nm, with a 66 nm shell that improved their stability and biocompatibility. The carbodiimide-activated coupling reaction was also used to decorate silica particles embedded with Cdots with 11-mercaptoundecanoid acid (MUA)-stabilized AuNCs (ø ~1.86 nm) [138].

MNCs can also contain more than one metal, in which case unravelling their structure and properties may become more complex. Babaee et al. prepared Au/Ag nanoclusters with the aid of insulin [(Ag/Au)@Ins], with overall diameter of 5.0 nm [136]. They further covalently bonded the bimetallic MNCs with Cdots by the EDAC/NHS protocol to obtain a dual emitting (440 nm and 640 nm) hybrid for selective detection of ions.

In situ synthesis. An elegant and straightforward method to prepare hybrids consists of the in situ formation of nanoclusters or quantum dots, which improves the interaction between both particles and circumvents complex multistep syntheses. In 2016, Zhang et al. reported the in situ preparation of AuNCs/Cdots hybrids by adding glucose to a dispersion of GSH-AuNCs and subsequently heating this mixture in a microwave oven [139]. The hybrid with dual emission bands centered at 470 nm (Cdots) and 565 nm (AuNCs) was composed of very small AuNCs embedded in a carbon skeleton, with overall size of 2 nm. Similar strategies to form in situ Cdots after synthesis of gold seeds were followed by others using glucose [140–142] or waste red onion leaves [143] as carbon precursors.

Alternatively, Cdots can be used as a reducing agent to form MNCs seeds from a metal ion precursor. Mathew et al. employed wheat gluten protein as carbon source to obtain stable blue-green emitting Cdots and subsequently conjugated them with free gluten protein to act as reducing agent to produce in situ red-emitting AuNCs [144]. The hybrid was composed of functionalized Cdots (ø ~ 3 nm) surrounded by AuNCs (ø > 1.5 nm) bound to their surfaces. Interestingly, by changing the concentration of HAuCl4 added to the gluten-functionalized Cdots, near white light-emitting AuNCs/Cdots were produced. Following similar procedures, Hu et al. produced carbon nitride quantum dots (CNdots) functionalized with BSA to form in situ BSA-AuNCs/CNdots, where the BSA-CNdots acted both as reductant and stabilizer [145]. The hybrid showed dual emission bands (450 nm and 650 nm) under excitation at 365 nm, and average ø ~ 5.6 nm. Furthermore, CuNCs were also formed in situ using Cdots as reducer/stabilizer [146,147].

Functionalized Cdots were also implemented as templates to aid the formation of MNCs. For instance, Xie et al. produced Cdots from phenylboronic acid, functionalized with BSA, which were applied as anchoring sites for the in situ formation of BSA-AuNCs [148]. The nanohybrid had an average ø ~ 2–3 nm and exhibited dual fluorescence bands centered at 440 and 655 nm upon excitation at 360 nm. Likewise, Wang et al. prepared Cdots with ø ~ 2.3 nm and functionalized them with double-stranded DNA to be further used as a template for the synthesis of CuNCs by reduction of Cu(II) in the presence of the ascorbate ion [147]. Formation of nanoclusters with ø ~ 2.5 nm was confirmed by TEM images. The hybrid showed dual-emission bands centered at 446 nm (Cdots) and 594 nm (CuNCs). A similar process has been recently described by Li et al. for the preparation of CuNCs/Cdots hybrids with double emission bands centered at 468 nm (Cdots) and 632 nm (CuNCs) [146].

Despite the successful in situ syntheses of MNCs using Cdots as reductant and stabilizer, the main challenge rests in the inhibition of the nanoclusters growth into nanoparticles. In this context, Jin et al. proposed a method to produce larger AgNPs using Cdots as reductant and then chemically etch the resulting particles to obtain smaller AgNCs [149]. Reduction of Ag(I) to Ag(0) occurred through the O-containing functional groups of the Cdots and the AgNPs formation was confirmed by the surface plasmon resonance (SPR) band at 420 nm. The AgNPs were then etched by NH4OH in ethanol at 80 °C to form smaller red-emitting AgNCs upon UV excitation.

Other syntheses of MNC/Cdots hybrids involved the formation of complexes with a third material, such as an analyte of interest [150] or a templating material, like metal organic frameworks (MOFS) [151,152] or TiO2 nanotubes [153]. Those methods may encompass one or more of the aforementioned synthetic paths.

Hybrids of MNCs and CNTs offer distinct possibilities as they can incorporate the optical and catalytic properties of MNCs and the remarkable properties of CNTs, such as stiffness, flexibility, chemical stability, electronic conduction and heat transport, among others [92].

Non-covalent interactions. MNCs can be functionalized to interact with the surface of CNTs through π-π stacking. Liu et al. resorted to such strategy to synthesize AuNCs/CNTs hybrids [154]. They first produced lysozyme-capped AuNCs (Lys-AuNCs, ø ~ 1.8 nm) by a microwave-assisted method, functionalized them with single-stranded DNA (ssDNA) and then mixed them with CNTs to yield the hybrid through π-π stacking. Overlapping of the Cdots emission with the CNTs absorption promoted FRET from the Lys-AuNCs to the CNTs. However, poor dispersibility of the AuNCs in the CNTs limited the use of the hybrid in FRET bioassays and imaging diagnosis [154]. Similarly, Jiang et al. assembled a hybrid of DNA-AgNCs (emission at 620 nm upon excitation at 568 nm) and SWCNTs [58]. After mixing with SWCNTs, DNA-AgNCs entwined onto the surface of the nanotubes through π-π stacking between the aromatic nucleotide bases and SWCNTs sidewalls.

In an innovative approach, Zhuang et al. developed bifunctional electrocatalyst based on ligand-free PdNCs of 5 atoms loaded onto CNTs [69]. They prepared PdNCs from PdCl2 using dimethylformamide (DMF) as solvent and reducing agent. To obtain smaller clusters, a ligand-exchange reaction with 1-dodecanethiol was performed, and the final Pd5(C12H25S)13 NCs were extracted with dichloromethane. The NCs were then incubated with MWCNTs dispersed in dichloromethane, washed and calcinated to obtain ligand-free clusters supported on the nanotubes. HRTEM showed that these clusters were nearly monodisperse with ø < 1 nm and that after calcination no aggregation occurred at the CNTs surface, which was attributed to the ultra-low loading of Pd (2%, as determined by ICP-AES).

Baharudin et al. performed the synthesis of CuNCs/CNTs hybrids via wet impregnation [155–157]. They synthesized catalysts based on phosphine-protected hexameric copper hydride nanoclusters [(PPh3)CuH]6, deposited on carboxylated-MWCNTs. A dispersion of [(PPh3)CuH]6 in tetrahydrofuran (THF) was added to MWCNTs in THF and impregnation proceeded by continuous stirring overnight [155]. Phosphine-protected CuNCs of about 2–3 nm diameter were produced, for a copper content ≈ 0.5–1% wt. Noncovalent interactions between CuNCs and MWCNTs proved to be stronger for the smaller nanoclusters.

Peng et al. proposed an innovative path for the fabrication of an immunosensor using carboxylated MWCNTs functionalized with poly(diallyldimethylammonium) chloride with positive charge as a support for negatively charged BSA-AuNCs [158]. Their work also incorporated P,S-Cdots as electrochemical luminescent markers and a glass carbon electrode (GCE) to compose the sensing platform.

In situ synthesis. In situ formation of MNCs was employed to produce hybrids with CNTs. Yuan et al. used oxygen defects of CeO2/1-pyrenecarboxylic acid-functionalized MWCNTs (CeO2/PC-MWCNTs) as nucleation spots for the reduction of Au(III) to form in situ AuNCs [159]. The CeO2/PC-MWCNTs were dispersed in ethylene glycol and incubated upon adding HAuCl4. A comparative analysis, summarized in Fig. 2 , was performed to understand the role of CeO2 in the nucleation and growth of AuNCs, resorting to different ways to grow CeO2 onto PC-MWCNTs and pristine MWCNTs (namely, by microwave irradiation or by conventional NH3 methods) [159]. They observed that AuNCs grew preferentially onto CeO2 nanoparticles rather than on the surface of either PC-MWCNTs or MWCNTs, and that the nanoparticle size (smaller using the microwave method) played a critical role in the nucleation and growth of the clusters. TEM further showed that AuNCs grown onto CeO2/PC-MWCNTs by the microwave method had an average ø ~ 1.6 nm, while AuNPs of ø ~ 18.0 nm grew onto CeO2/PC-MWCNTs by the NH3 method. In both cases, AuNCs or AuNPs were distributed mostly in the vicinities of the CeO2 nanoparticles owing to the tendency of gold to nucleate in oxygen defects.

The scarcity of experimental reports on MNC/CNT hybrids is indicative of the obstacles faced to achieve strong interactions between the two types of materials. Since the carbon atoms on the CNTs surface are chemically inert, surface activation (formation of anchoring sites) by chemical methods [160], e.g. wet oxidation in HNO3 [161], or physical processes, e.g. treatment with oxygen plasma [162] or electron/ion/laser irradiation [163], is required to attach the metal clusters. In this respect, computational studies on MNC formation at CNT surfaces provide valuable insight on how to tune the nature and density of defects to control the density and morphology of MNCs, and to further understand how CNTs can be used as templates to grow novel heterostructures.

In silico methods. Charlier et al. studied the interactions between Au atoms and CNTs surfaces [18]. DFT/LDA calculations predicted the binding energy of a single Au atom to a pristine surface of sp2 carbon atoms (Fig. 3 I). Given the low binding energy found (< 0.2 eV), isolated Au atoms on the surface would easily move, be trapped by surface defects (such as oxygenated vacancies or divacancies) and find other Au atoms to form clusters. Charge transfer between Au12 clusters and pristine CNTs was found to reduce the band gap of the semiconducting tubes and to open a small gap on the metallic ones. Conversely, smaller AuNCs (Au3 and Au5) interacted with bundles of either tube type, forming chains along the tube axis, which for semiconducting CNTs induced weak metallic character. The same authors showed by TEM that the formation of defects on MWCNTs surface could control the spatial dispersion and size of the surface-attached metal particles (Fig. 3 II).

Mixtures of AgNCs and CNTs were also addressed in silico by Akbarzadeh et al. [57]. MD simulations were carried out to investigate the thermal, structural, and dynamic properties of SWCNTs with AgNCs of different sizes on their outer surface, under various conditions of temperature, reaction time and pressure. The melting point of AgNCs on CNTs depended on the number of atoms and the contact point between the materials. For temperatures above the melting point, the clusters were flattened on the CNTs and rolled up around the nanotube leading to surface wetting. More recently, Duchêne et al. employed DFT to study the electronic structures of AgNCs of various sizes adsorbed on CNTs, as well as quantum size effects related with Ag/CNT interfaces [61]. The adsorption of AgNCs on CNTs induced a significant dipole moment at the interface due to electron charge transfer from the cluster to the nanotube. In addition, the AgNCs showed different adsorption energies, depending on their size, with chemisorption observed for smaller clusters (4 and 13 atoms) and physisorption (weaker) for larger ones (55 and 147 atoms). They also evaluated the adsorption of CO on the Ag/CNT interface, aiming at applications in catalysis, concluding that adsorption of CO by AgNCs weakened their interaction with CNTs, which could result in cluster fusion into larger particles.

One of the first works published on CuNCs/CNTs hybrids was carried out by Hwang et al., whereby they investigated by MD simulations the CuNC diffusion inside CNTs [16]. They proposed a growth mechanism of copper nanowires of 26 atoms inside ultrathin (5,5) CNTs. Other research groups used in silico experiments to unravel different properties of CNTs encapsulating CuNCs [164,165].

Nanoclusters of noble metals other than Au and Ag have also been assembled with CNTs to form novel materials with enhanced properties. Among them, PtNCs and PdNCs have been examined in the past years, in view of their excellent catalytic properties [166]. Cheng et al. reported Monte Carlo simulations on the thermal properties of icosahedral PtNCs of 55 Pt atoms encapsulated in SWCNTs [17]. The nanoclusters were placed initially at the center of the cross-section of the SWCNTs, with the temperature increasing from 100 K to 600 K. The structural transformations upon heating were simulated and assessed by heat capacities and potential energies. The results indicated melting-like structural transformations for the clusters inside SWCNTs, dependent on the pore size and temperature. Later on, the same group addressed the thermal behavior of PdNCs inside CNTs by MD simulations [68]. In this case, they considered SWCNTs with various chiralities and icosahedral PdNCs. To investigate the thermal behavior of the PdNCs/CNTs hybrids, they modelled the C–C, Pd–Pd, and Pd–Cl interaction energies and distances, in a temperature range including the melting point. They found that the melting temperature of PdNCs supported on SWCNTs was much lower than that of free clusters and that it was independent of the diameter of the SWCNTs.

Hybrids of MNCs with 2D CNMs have attracted great interest for catalysis, owing mainly to the large specific surface area and electronic properties afforded by the 2D nanocarbon [167]. Moreover, the emissive properties of MNCs and their quenching by the CNM substrate have been explored for sensing applications [168]. Graphene oxide (GO), graphene and graphitic carbon nitride nanosheets (g-C3N4) are the most employed materials.

Non-covalent interactions. The most common approach relies on preparing separate dispersions of MNCs and 2D carbon nanomaterial and mixing them to form hybrids via non-covalent interactions (electrostatic interactions, hydrogen bonding and π-π stacking). Optical sensors and catalysts based on non-covalent hybrids formed between MNCs and bare GO, reduced GO (rGO) or modified GO, with NH3 +, C6NH2, NH2 or ethylenediamine (EDA) groups, have become rather favored. Xiaofei et al. prepared hybrids composed of two types of AuNCs and GO [169,170]. First, they obtained dual AuNCs (D-AuNCs) made of negatively charged red-emitting BSA-capped AuNCs and positively charged blue-emitting Lys-AuNCs. The final D-AuNCs showed an overall negative charge due to the higher amount of BSA-AuNCs. Energy dispersive X-ray analysis showed that the Au(0) core in the D-AuNCs was ø ~ 1–2 nm, with the Lys-AuNCs residing at the surface of BSA-AuNCs. Finally, they functionalized GO with amine groups (GO-NH3 +) and mixed them with D-AuNCs to form the D-AuNCs/GO hybrid through electrostatic adsorption. The presence of Lys-AuNCs increased the fluorescence intensity of BSA-AuNCs, improving the response towards the analyte.

Likewise, Tao et al. mixed dispersions of positively charged Lys-AuNCs (ζ = +35.94 mV) and negatively charged GO sheets functionalized with folic acid (FA) (ζ = −50.20 mV) to obtain a hybrid for sensing [171]. The hybrid was explored as a nanosensor for cancer cells through interaction with folate receptors (Fig. 4 I). TEM showed that the AuNCs were well dispersed onto the GO sheets without the formation of large aggregates (Fig. 4 II). In another study, Wang et al. prepared a hybrid of transferrin-capped AuNCs (Tf-AuNCs) of ø ~ 2.6 nm and GO also by electrostatic interactions [21].

Wen et al. produced two different protein-stabilized AuNCs to study the influence of the ligand on the quenching by GO by electron transfer [172]. Using a conventional bottom-up procedure, they synthesized BSA-AuNCs with 25 Au atoms (λem = 650 nm) and His-AuNCs with 10 Au atoms (λem = 530 nm). The photoluminescence (PL) of both Au clusters was quenched by the GO dispersion. GO was also employed as support for protein-stabilized AgNCs [173]. The HSA-AgNCs and BSA-AgNCs clusters showed hydrodynamic radii of 2.6 nm and 2.0 nm and emission bands centered at 620 nm (λex = 480 nm) and 510 nm (λex = 440 nm), respectively.

Similarly to GO and GO-NH3 +, g-C3N4 modified with cationic amine groups were mixed with a solution of negatively charged BSA-AuNCs to fabricate a fluorescent probe for the detection of biomolecules [174]. Bulk g-C3N4 was synthesized by pyrolysis of urea, then protonated with HCl, exfoliated with ultrasonic treatment and aminated with ethylenediamine through the EDAC/NHS coupling reaction. The hybrid of aminated g-C3N4 and BSA-AuNCs was assembled through electrostatic interactions and exhibited dual fluorescence emission, on the blue at 436 nm from the nanosheets and on the red at 610 nm from the nanoclusters.

Hybrid formation can also involve hydrogen bonding between the individual components in addition to electrostatic interactions. Xu et al. prepared AuNCs/GO hybrids with AuNCs stabilized by human serum albumin (HSA-AuNCs) and GO bearing amine groups on the surface created by activation with SOCl2 in DMF and further reaction with hexylenediamine [175]. The hybrids showed dual emission from the hexylenediamine-functionalized GO (GO-C6NH2) at 450 nm and from the AuNCs at 670 nm.

The emission of MNCs can be quenched by energy and/or electron transfer to the 2D carbon nanomaterials substrate [176]. This effect has been effectively explored in the preparation of nanohybrid sensors for the detection of biomolecules [177–180] and ions [181–183] via simple mixing of hybrid-containing dispersions and the analyte of interest.

Conversely, while the MNCs emission is likely quenched by the substrate, the electrochemical and catalytic properties of MNCs can be enhanced by non-covalent interaction with GO. Zhao et al. synthesized 1.7 nm diameter hybrids MUA-AuNCs and GO, and after depositing them onto gold electrodes found improved photoelectrical and sensing properties, caused by the electron transfer from GO to the photoexcited holes of AuNCs [184]. In a related study, AuNCs stabilized by different ligands (BSA, GSH and DHLA) were used to form hybrids with GO, which were later deposited onto dithiol-modified gold electrodes [185]. In all cases, electron transfer from GO to AuNCs was observed.

Kwak et al. prepared hybrids with AuNCs of well-defined atomic composition and rGO. [186]. The hybrid containing hexanethiolate-protected AuNCs with 25 Au atoms supported onto rGO sheets was applied in electrocatalysis. GO was first reduced by PEI and the rGO was dispersed in ethanol and mixed with the clusters dispersed in dichloromethane. The resulting hybrids were then drop-casted onto GCE. PEI worked as GO-reducer, surface-modifier, and stabilizer of the AuNCs/rGO hybrids in the glassy carbon electrode (GCE). Other groups deposited MNC/2D nanocarbon hybrids onto the surfaces of electrodes to build up electrochemical biosensors, but since they did not perform optical characterizations the presence of nanoclusters in the hybrids remained unclear [19,187,188].

Hybrids prepared from 6-mercaptohexanoic acid (MHA)-protected AuNCs of well-defined atomic composition (Au25(MHA)18) and rGO were also employed to fabricate hybrid membranes for continuous-flow catalysis [189]. Reduced GO was synthesized via a hydrothermal method resorting to overheated supercritical deionized water as reducing agent. Both MHA-AuNCs and rGO dispersions were slightly negatively charged, which indicated that hydrophobic interactions played a major role in hybrid assembly.

Another popular approach to prepare MNC/2D nanocarbon hybrids involves π-π stacking. Hybrids of ssDNA-stabilized AgNCs and GO produced through π-π stacking between the hexagonal graphitic units of GO and the aromatic nucleic bases on ssDNA were studied by different groups [55,190]. In an original approach, Shamsipur et al. obtained hybrids of DNA-AgNCs and GO that showed increased emission upon interaction with GO, explained by a change in the amount of Ag (I)-DNA complexes in the outer shell of the AgNCs due to the oxidizing properties of GO [190].

Hybrids of CuNCs and 2D CNMs are still very recent in the literature, compared to those based on Au or Ag nanoclusters. In 2019, Liu et al. reported the formation of a hybrid of dithiothreitol (DTT)-functionalized CuNCs assembled onto carbon nitride nanosheets g-C3N4 by mixing dispersions of both nanomaterials [191]. TEM showed CuNCs deposited on g-C3N4, and double-emission at 450–460 nm (g-C3N4) and 615 nm (DTT-CuNCs) was found. Other routes to produce MNC/2D CNM hybrids via non-covalent binding included a third material as bridge or support [192,193].

Covalent bonding. Covalently bonded hybrids of MNCs and 2D CNMs have been more scarcely reported [194,195]. Zheng et al. produced a AuNCs/GO hybrid via the EDAC/NHS coupling reaction [195]. First, biligand-protected AuNCs were produced using MHA and cysteamine (Cystm) as ligands, and a GO dispersion was prepared with Ho(NO3)3 to obtain Ho-GO nanosheets with controllable orientation. The Au25Cystm14MHA17–14 clusters were then conjugated onto Ho-GO through EDAC/NHS coupling reaction. In a rather innovative approach, Bosch-Navarro et al. prepared hybrids between phosphine-coordinated AuNCs of 9 atoms, [Au9(PPh3)8](NO3), and sulfur-doped rGO [194]. The nanohybrid was produced by covalently binding the rGO and the AuNC by forming an Au–S bond upon displacement of one of the phosphine ligands. TEM demonstrated that the clusters were rotating under the influence of the electron beam without lateral displacement, indicating strong covalent Au–S bonds between the two nanomaterials.

In situ synthesis. Similarly to 0D and 1D CNMs, the in situ formation of MNCs has been also abundantly applied to prepare hybrids with 2D CNMs [20,59,196–200]. Jin et al. prepared hybrids of DNA-AgNCs onto N-doped GO sheets via in situ electrochemical deposition [59]. The hybrid was formed on a polished GCE surface by deposition of dispersed N-GO followed by electrochemical reduction of AgNO3 at the GO surface in presence of ssDNA (Fig. 5 I). The AgNCs with ø ~ 1.8 nm were well distributed onto the GO sheets in reticulate structures grown along the ssDNA template (Fig. 5 II).

Further, Wang et al. mixed graphene dispersed in DMF and NH3.H2O with HAuCl4 to form in situ AuNCs of ø ~ 1.4 nm [196], while Fujigaya et al. employed polymer-coated graphene to form AuNCs of different sizes (ø = 1.6, 3.3, 4.5 nm) with the aid of NaBH4 and ethylene glycol [197]. Song et al. reported the functionalization of GO with GSH to be used in the thermal co-reduction of Au(III) to AuNCs and GO to rGO [198]. The mechanism involved first the adsorption of Au(III) ions onto GO and then the formation of nucleation centers. GSH was used to facilitate the formation of GSH-AuNCs and reduction of GO into rGO. The final clusters were well-dispersed onto rGO with uniform size of ø ~ 1.4 nm.

Likewise, Griep et al. developed a synthetic route to prepare AuNCs in situ by using a previously BSA-functionalized rGO as reducing agent under alkaline conditions [199]. The hybrids showed the characteristic red emission of BSA-AuNCs of 25 Au atoms and an average ø ~ 1–1.25 nm. The direct synthesis of BSA-AuNCs onto BSA-functionalized GO proved to be a superior approach to obtain rGO and control the size and stability of the nanoclusters, compared to the pre-synthesized BSA-AuNCs adsorbed onto rGO sheets.

Alternatively, Yin et al. resorted to a one-pot approach to synthesize MNCs-rGO hybrids [20,200]. In an early work, they produced in situ AuNCs of ø ~ 1.8 nm from HAuCl4 and rGO as reducing agent [20]. Later, they prepared CuNCs in situ by adding GSH-functionalized rGO to a solution of Cu(II) in ethanol, with constant dropping of an aqueous solution of GSH. HRTEM showed hybrids of ø ~ 20 nm, with embedded CuNCs of ø ~ 2.0–2.5 nm [200]. Absence of the SPR absorption band in UV–Vis spectrum excluded the presence of CuNPs.

Hybrids of PtNCs and graphene nanosheets (GNS) have also been prepared in situ [65]. GNS (10–20 layers) was produced from GO using exfoliation and chemical reduction. A platinum precursor [Pt(NO2)2·(NH3)2] was mixed with GNS dispersion and subjected to a heating treatment in Ar/H2 mixture to obtain the PtNCs-GNS hybrid. The final Pt content was estimated as 20 wt% by ICP analysis. High-angle annular dark-field STEM (HAADF-STEM) showed two size distributions of PtNCs, one below 0.5 nm (consisting of 10–15 Pt atoms), and the other around 1.5 nm. The presence of those extremely small PtNCs suggested a strong interaction between Pt and GNS.

In silico methods. Computational methods have not been commonly applied to investigate the MNC/2D nanocarbon hybrids. Still, a few studies on PtNCs/graphene hybrids were reported in the past years, focusing mainly on the catalytic activity of platinum enhanced by electron transfer from graphene [66,67]. Additionally, Jalili et al. used DFT calculations to study Ag8 clusters supported onto 3 different types of N-Gr, namely graphitic-N1 (or G-N1), Pyrrole-N1 (P–N1) and pyridinic-N3 (P–N3) [201]. Among these, the Ag8 NC with a vertex atom in the P–N3 position was the most stable and efficient hybrid for the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR), as this configuration was the best to stabilize the cluster.

One of the first experimental investigations on CNM-supported MNCs was the study of quenching by electron transfer in BSA-protected Au25 (BSA–Au25) and histidine-protected Au10 (His–Au10) supported on GO [172]. It was found that the PL emission of AuNCs was quenched in the hybrids due to an ultrafast electron transfer from GO to the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) of the metal clusters. This ultrafast photoinduced electron transfer paved the way for applications of these hybrids in photovoltaics, photonics and photocatalysis. In particular, and as we discuss in the next subsection, applications in luminescence sensing have been based on the modulation of the electron transfer between MNCs and CNMs, and the consequent turn-off-and-on PL emission resulting from the separation between the clusters and the nanocarbon surface triggered by an analyte (Scheme 7c).

The following discussion has been divided into optical and electrochemical sensing. Within the optical sensing methods separate sections deal with the detection of biomacromolecules (proteins, enzymes, and DNA), small molecules and ions.

Luminescence sensors have been developed to explore the PL emission of MNCs, usually in the red to NIR regions. The presence of two moieties in the hybrid allows the development of ratiometric sensors that improve the sensitivity and accuracy of detection, by avoiding the influence of probe concentration and experimental artifacts such as excitation power fluctuations. The working principle behind the ratiometric sensing relies on the existence of two well-separated PL emission bands, whose relative intensity changes due to the presence of an analyte. Typically, the PL emission of the Cdots in the blue (400–500 nm) is relatively insensitive to the analyte, while the red emission of the MNCs (600–700 nm) is quenched due to either etching, photoinduced charge transfer, energy transfer or complexation by the analyte (Scheme 7a) [202–205]. The opposite effect, i.e. having the emission of the Cdots sensitive to the analyte and that of MNCs insensitive, even though unusual, has also been reported [206]. It is worth noting that, when FRET occurs between Cdots and MNCs, the PL of the donor (Cdots) is reduced while the acceptor (MNC) emission is enhanced. The presence of an analyte that inhibits the FRET process promotes the gradual recovery of the emission of the Cdots and the decrease of the emission of the MNC (due to the suppression of its indirect excitation by FRET), as shown in Scheme 7b. On the other hand, MNCs supported on carbon-based 2D nanomaterials generally work as single emission fluorescent sensors, with the emission of the nanoclusters sensitive to the analyte. The mechanism of sensing relies on the quenching (turn-off) of the PL emission of the MNCs, due to the interaction with the CNM substrate. When the analyte has the ability to displace the MNCs from the CNM surface, the emission of the nanoclusters is gradually recovered (turn-on). The working principle of this turn-off-and-on strategy is shown in Scheme 7c.

The mechanisms of optical sensing illustrated in Scheme 7 are the most common ones for MNC/CNM hybrids, even though they are not exclusive. Throughout this section, the sensing of proteins and DNA, small molecules and ions will be considered, exploring selected examples of the implementation of such mechanisms.

Proteins, and specifically enzymes, play a pivotal role in most biological processes. Recently, a significant number of protein- and enzyme-sensing methods based on functionalized AuNCs have been developed with remarkable selectivity and sensitivity [207]. As a natural development of this research topic, hybrids of MNCs supported on CNMs have been targeted towards the sensing of biomacromolecules and cancer cells. To increase selectivity towards cancer cells, proteins are often incorporated in the nanohybrids [208].

Nanohybrids of AuNCs embedded in Cdots were used for imaging and as a ratiometric fluorescence sensor to monitor Fe3+ level in normal rat osteoblast cells. The red emission of the AuNCs is quenched by the analyte while the blue emission of the carbon core remained almost unaffected [139]. Active targeting allowed to extend this approach to several biomolecules, such as trypsin inhibitor protein [145,199], T4 polynucleotide kinase [147], and traumatic brain injury biomarker S100B [142].

Ultra-sensitive detection of trypsin inhibitor protein (TI) was developed based on BSA-AuNCs/CNdots hybrids [145]. BSA-protected CNdots were used as both a reductant and a stabilizer for the synthesis of BSA-AuNCs/CNdots. The hydrolysis of BSA occurred in the presence of trypsin, leading to decomposition of AuNCs and consequent decrease of the PL emission at 650 nm, while the Cdots PL emission at 450 nm remained unchanged. The presence of TI prevented BSA hydrolysis and quenching of the PL of the AuNCs. The hydrolysis of BSA by trypsin was further used to directly detect trypsin using a turn-off sensor based on BSA-AuNCs supported on rGO [199].

Dual-emitting CuNCs/Cdots hybrids were developed as ratiometric fluorescence nanosensor for monitoring T4 polynucleotide kinase (PNK) activity [147]. The formation of CuNCs was assisted by dsDNA-functionalized Cdots. The phosphorylation of dsDNA by T4 PNK, and subsequent degradation, was shown to destabilize the CuNCs. Thus, the PL emission of CuNCs at 594 nm was quenched in the presence of T4 PNK, while the PL emission of Cdots at 446 nm remained unchanged. This approach to monitor the activity of T4 PNK was validated in a complex real sample (matrix of cell lysates).

A ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobe for detection of mild traumatic brain injury (mTBI) biomarker S100B was developed based on AuNCs embedded in Cdots functionalized by a capture peptide (Cys-TRTK-12) [142]. The AuNCs-embedded Cdots were functionalized with the Cys-TRTK-12 peptide via Au–S covalent bonds. The peptide had a good selectivity towards calcium-activated S100B protein. The authors claimed that the quenching of the AuNCs PL emission at 580 nm was triggered by the interaction of the hydrophobic residue of the peptide with the S100B protein, involving a charge transfer from the AuNCs to the complex. The florescence intensity of Cdots (460 nm) remained constant and was used as internal reference. Satisfactory and fast (30 min) results were obtained in the S100B detection in spiked blood samples. Besides disease diagnosis, this biomarker approach bears great potential in forensic science.

CNTs have also been used as a substrate for MNCs in sensing. Lys-AuNCs supported on CNTs were used to develop a tun-off-and-on fluorescent sensor for microRNA-21 detection [154]. MicroRNA-21 has been identified as a biomarker and a target for cancer treatment. The Lys-AuNCs were modified with the ss-DNA probe for microRNA-21 and later adsorbed at the surface of the CNTs by π–π stacking interactions between aromatic nucleotide bases and CNTs. The adsorption onto the surface of CNTs caused the quenching of the orange-red PL emission of the ssDNA-Lys-AuNCs (610 nm). The PL emission was recovered after addition of microRNA-21 due to hybridization of the ss-DNA with the microRNA-21 that led to desorption of the ss-DNA-Lys-AuNCs from the surface of the CNTs. The sensor had a linear response in a wide concentration range (0.01 to 100 nM) and a limit of detection (LOD) of 36 pM. It was possible to detect microRNA-21 in human serum and to distinguish normal and cancer cells by direct imaging of intracellular microRNA-21 in living cells, which demonstrated the high potential of this sensor for clinical diagnosis.

A related approach was followed to quantify and detect HeLa cancer cells using AgNCs/SWCNTs hybrids [58]. The PL emission at 620 nm of AgNCs capped with an oligonucleotide S2 (AgNCs-S2) was quenched upon adsorption at the SWCNT sidewalls (≤ 5 nm, and lengths 5–15 μm). Cancer cells overexpressing the folate receptors (FRs) were incubated with a solution of folic acid (FA) bound to the complementary oligonucleotide sequence (FA-S1). The interaction of AgNCs-S2/SWCNTs with FRs-bound FA-S1 lead to recovery of the PL emission of AgNCs due to hybridization between S1 and S2, unwrapping the AuNC-S2 from the SWCNT surface. The relatively poor dispersibility of the CNTs was found to be the main limitation of the method [58,154].

The intrinsic enzyme-mimetic properties of CNM-supported AuNCs have been the key feature in the development of many sensing methods. One of the earliest works exploring such properties took advantage of the catalytic peroxidase activity of the AuNCs/GO hybrid with a folate linker for targeting FR overexpressed at the surface of cancer cells (as shown in Fig. 4) [171]. The FA-AuNCs/GO hybrid exhibited selective, quantitative and fast colorimetric detection of cancer cell lines (HeLa and MCF-7) signaled by the appearance of a green color due to catalytic oxidation of 3,3′,5,5′-Tetramethylbenzidine (TMBOx) in the presence of H2O2. Noteworthy, both bare AuNCs and GO were almost catalytically inactive.

Wang et al. reported that transferrin (Tf)-functionalized AuNCs supported on GO sheets (Tf-AuNCs/GO) acted as a turn-off-and-on NIR fluorescent probe for cancer cells, overexpressing the transferrin receptor (TfR) (Fig. 6 I) [21]. The sensing methodology relied on the quenching (turn-off) of NIR emission of the Tf-AuNCs at 710 nm (Fig. 6 II) due to the interaction with the GO substrate. The fluorescence was restored (turn-on) in the presence of TfR due to the stronger interaction between Tf and TfR when compared with the interaction between Tf and GO (Fig. 6 I). The final hybrid had high specificity and affinity towards TfR, both in 2D cultured cells and in small animal models (Fig. 6 III), with negligible cytotoxicity.

A similar strategy was used for the development of ssDNA-AgNCs and GO hybrids employed as turn-off-and-on fluorescent DNA sensors, aptasensors, and multiplex sensors for the detection of genes of harmful infectious pathogens [55], cytochrome C [190], and adenosine triphosphate (ATP) [190]. The fluorescence of ssDNA–AgNCs was quenched by energy/electron transfer from AgNCs to GO, when adsorbed onto GO sheets. Binding of the target ssDNA-AgNCs to the complementary strand of the probe DNA yielded a duplex with no affinity towards GO. The fluorescence of the AgNCs was recovered upon desorption from the GO surface. Multiplex detection was implemented using sequence-specific stabilized AgNCs with red- and NIR-emitting DNA-AgNCs (616 and 775 nm). [55].

Turn-off-and-on sensors have also been used to monitor protein kinase activity in cells [179]. Red emissive MUA-AuNCs were linked via Au–S bonds to a specifically designed peptide bearing a terminal cysteine. The peptide had two positively charged arginine residues to promote electrostatic interactions between GO and AuNCs, followed by quenching of PL emission of MUA-AuNCs. Recovery of the PL emission was induced by reducing the affinity of MUA-AuNCs towards GO upon phosphorylation of a specific sequence within the peptide that worked as the substrate for protein kinase A. The applicability of the method was further expanded to assess the H-89 kinase inhibition with a half-maximal inhibitory concentration of 0.049 μmol.L−1. A similar design strategy for the linker peptide was adopted to develop AuNCs/GO-based turn-off-and-on fluorescence probe for the detection of matrix metalloproteinase-9 (MMP-9) secreted from human breast adenocarcinoma MCF-7 cells [178]. The PL of the peptide-MUA-AuNCs at 640 nm was quenched upon loading onto GO. The recovery of PL emission of the AuNCs occurred in the presence of MMP-9 enzyme due to hydrolysis of the surface peptide followed by the released of the peptide-MUA-AuNCs from the GO surface.

In another type of application, heparin, a naturally occurring biopolymer widely used as anticoagulant to prevent thrombosis, was probed by AuNCs or CuNCs supported in NH2-GO or rGO, respectively. The negatively charged heparin interacted with NH2-GO or rGO and decreased the initial quenching of the luminescence of the MNCs by the support, resulting in a heparin turn-on sensor [177,200].

In general, the described approaches for enzyme detection displayed good sensitivity, selectivity, and simplicity. The limitations of the method are related with the specific requirements of a DNA sequence and the need of an extra enzyme. Table 1 summarizes the most relevant protein- and enzyme-sensing methods based on functionalized MNCs.

Reactive oxygen species (ROS) are involved in normal cell functions at biologically tolerated levels. However, at higher concentrations, ROS are poisonous due to the oxidation of proteins, DNA or lipids. The fluorescence of AuNCs supported in Cdots can be successfully applied in the real-time tracking of oxidative stress levels in both in vitro and in vivo models [128]. Binary heterogeneous nanocomplexes synthesized from GSH-AuNCs coupled with Cdots have been shown to be resistant to photobleaching, while signaling the presence of ROS in living cells with high contrast [128]. The ratiometric sensor was based on the reduction in luminescence of AuNCs due to their oxidation by ROS, while the PL emission of the Cdots remained unaffected. The nanosensor was tested in murine macrophage cells and applied in the detection of ROS in mice with local ear inflammation, demonstrating its physiological stability and excellent biocompatibility.

A similar strategy was pursued for the ratiometric fluorescence sensing of H2O2 by nanocarbon-supported MNCs [47,122,127,133,137]. The principle for ratiometric sensing was based on the peroxidase-like activity of Cdots that produces hydroxyl radicals in the presence of H2O2. The red emission of the MNCs was quenched due to etching by the hydroxyl radicals, while the blue PL emission of the Cdots was relatively insensitive to the presence of H2O2, and hence used as a reference signal. A modified Fenton-assisted approach was also reported for the detection of H2O2 based on AuNCs/Cdots hybrids. [47,133]. A related method achieved dual sensing of H2O2 and ClO− [122]. Additionally, a multitarget sensor was developed for H2O2, doxycycline and I−, exploring possible interactions of the analytes not only with the AuNCs, but also with the carbon core and the stabilizing ligand [137]. In this case, Cdots were prepared from papain, that additionally stabilize the AuNCs. Both moieties were covalently linked to build a dual-channel sensor. While the H2O2 oxidized Au (0) to Au (I) causing the quenching of AuNCs PL, the interaction of the hybrid with I− quenched the PL of Cdots. The presence of tetracyclines reduced the PL of both Cdots and AuNCs by the inner filter effect (IFE).

Other metals than gold, although less common, are also used in sensing as it is the case of BSA-AgNCs mixed with N, Fe-doped Cdots for the detection of H2O2 and uric acid [127]. Uric acid levels in urine are commonly used to signal the presence of gout, kidney stones, hypertension, and other types of health issues. In the reported study, the detection of uric acid was based on its catalytic oxidation to H2O2 and 5-hydroxyisourate by uricase enzyme and subsequent formation of OH− assisted by the N, Fe-doped Cdots.

GSH-CuNCs/Cdots hybrids were developed for the ratiometric fluorescence sensing of water [118,119]. A tight control of water content is required in pharmaceutical manufacturing, food processing, and organic synthesis. The GSH-CuNCs/Cdots hybrid exhibited dual blue and orange emissions of Cdots and CuNCs aggregates, respectively. The mechanism for humidity detection was based on aggregation-induced emission (AIE) of CuNCs in different environments. In good solvents for CuNCs, such as water, the orange AIE emission was not observed, while poor solvents, such as ethanol, promoted CuNCs aggregation and the appearance of the orange emission. [118] This approach was used in the detection of atmospheric humidity using filter paper impregnated with the CuNCs/Cdots (Fig. 7 I). The sensor response depended on the solvent, but its potential was validated for the determination of H2O in medical alcohol and wine samples, using digital photographs to visually analyze the samples (Fig. 7 II) [119].

BSA-AuNCs or BSA-CuNCs decorating g-C3N4 or forming hybrids with Cdots were used as sensors for dopamine (DA) [132,174]. The strong blue emission of the AuNCs/g-C3N4 hybrid at 420 nm was quenched by FRET to the oxidized form of DA (quinone) at the surface of the AuNCs. In the case of the BSA-AuNCs/Cdots hybrid, DA quenched the BSA-AuNCs emission at 610 nm due to charge transfer to the oxidized DA while the Cdots emission at 420 nm recovered due to inhibition of the FRET processes [131].

An interesting turn-off-and-on sensor was proposed for the detection of ascorbic acid (AA) in the presence of Cd2+ using MUA-AuNCs/Cdots hybrids [129]. The AuNCs emission of the MUA-AuNCs/Cdots was quenched in the presence of Cd2+ due to formation of non-emissive cubic-shaped aggregates (though quenching was not observed when Cd2+ was added to either MUA-AuNCs or Cdots separately). Recovery of the emission of the MUA-AuNCs/Cdots occurred in the presence of AA, because of the preferential interaction of Cd2+ with AA leading to the disruption of the former aggregates. A similar pathway was proposed for the detection of bleomycin (an anti-tumor and antibiotic drug) based on the Cu2+-quenching of the GSH-AuNCs/N-Cdots hybrid emission [135], and for the detection of deferiprone (an anti-thalassemia drug) by the quenching of the AuNCs/Cdots hybrid emission by Fe3+ [124].

Two AuNCs/CNM hybrids were developed for the detection of glucose. One of them was built by the assembly of negatively charged glucose oxidase and AuNCs on the surface of GO-NH3 + [169]. The glucose oxidase promoted oxidation of glucose with the formation of H2O2. The oxidation of H2O2 occurred with the simultaneous reduction of Au+ to Au at the surface of the AuNCs with the consequent quenching of the AuNCs emission. The sensor was deemed competitive with the current glucose sensors with the advantage of not requiring calibration. Alternatively, a ratiometric sensor was proposed based on AuNCs/Cdots allowing for sensing of glucose and H2O2 in human serum samples [122]. Furthermore, the biosensor also responded to the decomposition of H2O2, detecting ROS and hypochlorite (ClO−) on the test paper.

The interaction of the thiols in L-Cys with GSH-AuNCs/Cdots or N-acetyl-L-Cysteine–capped AuNCs (NAC-AuNCs/Cdots) hybrids was exploited for the ratiometric detection of L-Cys in different biological media [117,121]. The sensing mechanism was associated with quenching of the PL emission of the AuNCs either by L-Cys etching of the AuNCs or reduction of the surface Au+ ions. A significant FRET from Cdots to AuNCs was observed. However, as the PL emission of the Cdots remained unchanged upon addition of L-Cys, the etching mechanism, that would destroy the AuNCs acceptors, is less plausible. BSA-AgNCs/N-Cdots hybrids were used for the detection of bio-thiols [60]. The lifetime of N-Cdots remained unchanged upon increasing the amount of AgNCs in a dispersion of N-Cdots indicating that the quenching is due to IFE of the Cdots luminescence by the AgNCs. Addition of L-Cys lead to the formation of Ag–S bonds with the consequent breakdown of the AgNCs and recovery of the emission of the Cdots.

The GSH-AuNCs/Cdots hybrids were further used as a dual sensor for Ag+ and L-Cys. The emission of AuNCs increased with the addition of Ag+ and was quenched by L-Cys [123]. AuNCs aggregation-enhanced emission by Ag+ and d10-d10 interaction between Au ions were proposed to explain the enhancement of the AuNCs emission in the presence of Ag+, while the quenching by L-Cys was attributed to the AuNCs etching [123]. Aggregation-induced quenching of AuNCs was the mechanism put forth to explain the detection of carbendazim by AuNCs/N-Cdots hybrids [126]. In this case, the PL intensity of N-Cdots was quenched by FRET to AuNCs, but it recovered in the presence of carbendazim that promoted aggregation of GSH-AuNCs. The aggregation was monitored by following the intensity of the 2nd order Rayleigh scattering.

AuNCs/Cdots hybrids were also used to detected hazardous materials of anthropogenic source found in water. A molecular-imprinted fluorescence sensor based on silica nanoparticles loaded with Cdots and AuNCs was developed for the detection of bisphenol A (BPA), a teratogenic molecule used to make plastics for food packaging [140].

Overall, most of the MNC/CNM hybrids used for the detection of small molecules are composed of Cdots, with a few examples of hybrids with 2D CNMs, and no report of MNC/CNT hybrids, as summarized in Table 2 . MNC/Cdot hybrids take advantage of the dual fluorescence emission of the hybrid to build ratiometric sensors, where the MNCs luminescence is sensitive to the analyte but not the Cdots fluorescence, which remains unchanged.

The release of heavy metals from human activities are one of the most hazardous and widespread water and soil contaminants. Due to their non-biodegradable and bio-accumulative properties, metallic ions are readily absorbed and accumulated by animals and plants. Their detection and quantification by MNCs (M = Au, Cu) supported on CNMs is based on specific metallophilic interactions, charge transfer processes and enhancement of the intersystem crossing.

The modification of AuNCs/Cdots with dithiothreitol (DTT) improved the sensitivity towards Hg2+ detection promoting the Hg2+-Au+ interactions [141]. The free thiol groups of DTT acted as a Hg2+ trap, attracting the metal ions to the DTT-AuNCs/Cdots surface. The detection was limited to pH values below 8.0 due to the DTT oxidation at basic pH. In order to improve stability, BSA-AuNCs/Cdots were co-encapsulated in zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (BSA-AuNCs/Cdots/ZIF-8) and used as a ratiometric sensor for Hg2+, with LOD =1 nM [152]. A functionalized bimetallic (Au/Ag) nanocluster was produced employing insulin (Ins) as both capping and reducing agent [136]. The (Au/Ag)NCs@Ins/Cdots hybrid was found to be highly selective towards Hg2+ and Cu2+ ions by pH control switching, between acidic (pH = 4.0) and basic (pH = 10.0), respectively.

One of the first reported strategies aimed at the detection of Cu2+ employed GSH-AuNCs/g-C3N4 and chitosan as a dual-emission sensor [192]. The emission of the GSH-AuNCs decreased with the increasing Cu2+ concentration, resulting in a color change from orange-red to blue. The fluorescence quenching was probably due to a redox reaction between Cu2+ ions and the ligands of the AuNCs. The GSH-AuNCs/ g-C3N4 probe selectivity towards Cu2+ was tested against Ca2+, Ba2+, Mn2+, Mg2+, Co2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, but Hg2+ was not considered as an interferent. Another strategy to probe Cu2+ was based on the use of GSH-AuNCs/Cdots embedded in MOFs (ZIF-8) that could greatly amplify the signal of the incorporated fluorescent nanomaterials [151]. The mechanism of the AuNCs turn-off fluorescence observed in the presence of Cu2+ appeared to be a combination of static and dynamic quenching. The static process was attributed to the coordination of the metal ion by the carboxylic group of GSH while the dynamic process, probed by the decrease in the fluorescence lifetime, resulted from the diffusion of free Cu2+ ions. The use of BSA-AuNCs/N-Cdots allowed also for the selective detection and monitoring of Cu2+ levels in HepG2 cells in the presence of many cations and amino acids [114,134]. Multimetal detection of Cu2+and Pb2+ was also achieved by using GSH-AuNCs/Cdots hybrids [115]. The AuNCs fluorescence was quenched by Cu2+ and enhanced by Pb2+, yet the mutual interference observed when both ions coexisted came as a serious drawback.
