Dual response to metal ions and biologically relevant molecules is an approach that can improve the selectivity. NAC-AuNCs with N,S,P co-doped Cdots hybrids were applied as a ratiometric sensor for Cu2+ and L-histidine based on FRET [120]. The far-red fluorescence of NAC-AuNCs was quenched in the presence of Cu2+ and recovered in the presence of L-His. The turn-off-and-on strategy was dependent on the NAC-AuNCs vs L-His affinity with Cu2+. A similar approach was adopted for the simultaneous detection of Cu2+ and alkaline phosphatase (ALP) by hybrids of N-Cdots/SiO2 nanoparticles with MUA-AuNCs [138]. In this case, the quenching induced by Cu2+ was modulated by the presence of pyrophosphate, for which Cu2+ had a higher affinity than the AuNCs. Pyrophosphate was catalytically hydrolysed into phosphate by ALP conferring a high selectivity to the sensor. Dual sensing of Ag+ and L-Cys by GSH-AuNCs/Cdots [123] and Cd2+ and AA by MUA-AuNCs/Cdots [129] was already considered in the small molecules section and so will not be further developed here.

A direct method to detect Pb2+ was developed based on GSH-CuNCs/N-Cdots hybrids [146]. In this case, the emission of the nanohybrid was largely dominated by the blue emission of the Cdots. In the presence of Pb2+, the emission of CuNCs was enhanced owing to AIE. The simultaneous detection of Pb2+ and Ag+ was afforded by hybrids composed of dual ion-imprinted polymers with GSH-AuNCs and amino-functionalized Cdots embedded [150]. Here, Pb2+ quenched the emission of AuNCs while Ag+ quenched the emission of amino-Cdots. The ion-imprinted polymer component not only eliminated the interference of other ions, resulting in remarkable selectivity, but also conferred high chemical stability to the sensor.

GO and its derivatives were used to develop turn-on fluorescent probes for the detection of Cd2+ [182,183]. GSH-AuNCs/HRGO [182] and GSH-AuNCs/EDA-GO [183] constituted different AuNCs/GO nanoplatforms with recognition properties for Cd2+. The AuNCs luminescence of the GSH-AuNCs/HRGO hybrid was quenched by the interactions with the CNM substrate, and afterwards recovered upon desorption of the AuNCs from the CNM surface promoted by in situ formation of Cd-AuNCs complexes [182]. For the GSH-AuNCs/EDA-GO hybrid, the AuNCs emission was quenched by Cu2+ and recovered by AIE promoted by Cd2+ [183]. The EDA-GO subtract was used to provide a higher color contrast to the sensor, while Cu2+ improved the response of the AuNCs upon addition of Cd2+.

Apart from metal cations, examples of detection of other ions are scarce and, to our knowledge, limited to I− and NO2 − [137,175]. Table 3 summarizes the main data regarding the detection of metal ions by MNC/CNM hybrids. Overall, the mechanism of sensing is based on the quenching (turn-off) or enhancement (turn-on) of PL intensities of either MNCs or CNMs.

Electrochemical sensing can be done in the dark due to spontaneous redox reactions taking place at the surface of an electrode, or it can be aided by light in a photoelectrochemical (PEC) process, whereby charge separation and injection take place in the excited state. PEC sensing involves an electrical signal generated by light in photoactive materials. On the other hand, electrochemiluminescence (ECL) sensing is a process by which light is emitted in response to an applied electric voltage. It involves the generation of charged species at electrode surfaces that later undergo electron-transfer reactions to form emissive excited states. ECL as an analytical method combines the simplicity and controllability of electrochemistry with the sensitivity, reduced background noise, and wide linear range of chemiluminescence. Nevertheless, when compared with optical sensing, ECL methods are more invasive and less suitable for in vivo monitoring.

The PEC sensing of H2O2 has been explored using a hybrid of AuNCs and GO deposited on a gold electrode (GE), to determine the presence of H2O2 extracted from living human umbilical vein endothelial cells stimulated by angiotensin II [184]. In this study, the intrinsic peroxidase-like activity of AuNCs, where H2O2 could be reduced by photoexcited electrons in the nanoclusters, was well established. The presence of GO improved the charge separation in the AuNCs and promoted the reduction of H2O2. The photoelectrical properties of the hybrid electrode were seen to depend on the GO concentration and number of layers. Compared to GE modified by AuNCs alone, the hybrid electrode displayed a more than ten-fold photocurrent enhancement at a working voltage of −500 mV (vs. Ag/AgCl) [184].

The direct determination of free reduced and total L-Cys in urine has been successfully carried out using a BSA-AuNCs/GO/GC electrode without the assistance of any separation techniques [19]. The BSA-AuNCs/GO composite had an important role in the electrocatalytic activity towards the oxidation of L-Cys. A low oxidation potential (+0.387 V) was achieved. Noteworthy, the presence of AuNCs was unclear, since the size of the AuNCs reported was around 6 nm without luminescence. Similar AuNCs were employed to build hybrids with either MWCNTs or GO to monitor biologically relevant organic molecules by electrochemical methods. [187,188].

ECL methods were developed for the detection of okadaic acid using uniform-sized P, S co-doped Cdots (P, S-Cdots) conjugated with a monoclonal antibody against okadaic acid (anti-OA-MAb) as the bright ECL signaling marker, and carboxylated MWCNT-poly(diallyldimethylammonium) chloride-AuNCs (MWCNT-PDDA-AuNCs) composite as the substrate for okadaic acid immobilization [158]. The AuNCs moiety in the MWCNT-PDDA-AuNCs substrate increased the surface area and promoted the electron transfer. ECL was also explored in hybrids of 2D carbon nitride nanosheets (g-C3N4) with CuNCs functionalized by dithiothreitol (DTT), which eventually resulted in a DTT-CuNCs/g-C3N4 probe towards Hg2+ detection [191]. DTT not only stabilized the surface of the CuNCs, but it also improved the sensitivity of Hg2+ detection, by facilitating the Hg2+ accumulation on the clusters surface and promoting the reaction with Cu2+. On the other hand, g-C3N4 improved the ECL intensity due to its remarkable photocatalytic properties. The ECL mechanism was dependent on the anion-radical species resulting from the electrochemical DTT-CuNCs/g-C3N4 reaction with K2S2O8. In the presence of Hg2+, this reaction was inhibited, leading to the quenching of the initial excited state.

The single example of electrochemical sensing by MNC/CNM hybrids that did not involve luminescence was based on modified GCE by graphene-stabilized AuNCs [196]. Aiming at selectivity, the hybrid was further functionalized with cytosine-rich capture aptamer, which could bind to gold atoms by the Au–N bond and intertwine with AuNCs to get the target near the electrode surface. A strategy with exonuclease III-assisted target recycling amplification was implemented for the detection of HIV DNA [196].
A summary of electrochemical sensors based on the combination of MNCs and CNMs, especially graphene-based materials, can be found in Table S1.

The catalytic properties of metals (particularly gold) in the form of nanoparticles and nanoclusters have become a topic of intense research in the last decade. The catalytic activity of metal clusters is generally higher than that of the analogous metal nanoparticles, which is attributed to the quantum size effects (when size <2 nm), higher specific surface area and morphology of the clusters [209,210]. CNMs are popular supports of metal catalysts, in view of the high number of reactive sites, electric conductivity, large specific surface area, and thermal and mechanical stability. MNCs are generally capped with appropriate ligands not only to control the size and polydispersity during the synthesis and but also to improve their stability towards dissolution, aggregation and sintering during the catalytic cycles. The choice of the CNM support for the clusters is pivotal for the formation and stabilization of the clusters, while affording synergistic catalytic activity. This section will cover the reactions catalyzed by MNCs supported in CNMs of various dimensions, with focus on oxidation reactions, reducing reactions, coupling reactions, production of H2 and the use of the MNC/CNM hybrid as nanoenzymes. Scheme 8 shows an overview of the catalytic applications according to the type of MNC/CNM hybrid.

Cu-based materials catalyze the CO conversion reactions, such as the low temperature water-gas shift (LTWGS) reaction and CO oxidation. Although it was known that Cu unsupported clusters catalyzed the CO oxidation, it was only in 2019 that Baharudin et al. reported that a hexameric copper (Cu6) hydride supported on carboxyl-functionalized multiwalled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTsCOOH) catalyzed efficiently the LTWGS reaction at temperatures (120–200 °C) below the working temperatures of industrial processes [155]. The active species was a Cu2O new phase, generated when the cluster hydride was supported in MWCNTsCOOH. The optimal loading was 1 wt%. Results showed a poor kinetic activity in the LTWGS reaction using alternative alumina as support when compared to MWCNTsCOOH. This was probably due to the fact that Cu-free pristine MWCNT were also catalytically active for CO oxidation starting at ~150 °C and approached a complete conversion of CO to CO2 at 230 °C [157]. More recently, it was reported that the Cu6/MWCNTsCOOH catalyst had a limited range of high operating temperatures for oxidation of CO in the presence of O2 [156]. On the basis of theoretical calculations, AgNCs supported on CNTs were also expected to be catalytically active in the oxidation of CO [61].

The oxidation of small organic molecules at fuel cells can be catalyzed by different MNC/CNM hybrids. Zhuang et al. prepared atomically precise Pd5 nanoclusters supported on MWCNTs (Pd5NCs/MWCNTs) for both the ethanol oxidation and oxygen reduction reactions [69]. When compared to the commercial Pd/C electrode, the Pd5NCs/MWCNTs-modified electrode had lower mass loading of Pd (2% vs. 5 wt%), higher mass and specific activity, and better durability. The Pd5NCs/MWCNTs-modified electrode also exhibited lower onset potential, lower peak potential, and lower charge transfer resistance. The better electrocatalytic performance of the supported Pd5NCs was attributed to its relatively high specific surface area and high propensity to adsorb the fuels and oxygen.

The valorization of glycerol through oxidation reactions into high value products is catalyzed by several metals (Pd, Pt, Bi and Au), with the rare and expensive Pt catalyst being the best suit. To replace Pt for the oxidation of glycerol, Yuan et al. used CeO2 nanoclusters of 1.5 nm and AuNCs (< 2 nm) supported on MWCNT [159]. AuNCs were the catalytic active species, while the presence of CeO2NCs favored the formation of a uniform dispersion of ultrafine AuNCs. Such hybrid electrocatalyst revealed excellent activity and durability at practical fuel cell operation conditions. When compared to the commercial Pt/C catalyst, both the peak current density and the current density were 29.5 and 1.6 times higher under practical fuel cell operation potential (−0.3 V vs. SCE), respectively.

Chen et al. used a non-noble metal sacrificial approach to deposit highly dispersed noble MNCs on rGO [211]. The sacrificial compound (Co3(BO3)2) aided the immobilization of AgPd clusters (2–4.5 nm) and prevented their aggregation. The Ag0.1Pd0.9/rGO catalyst exhibited high activity for the dehydrogenation of formic acid in sodium formate. The formic acid dehydrogenation reaction showed an average turnover frequency (TOF) of 2756 ± 180 h−1 at 323 K, comparable to the higher value reported for the analogous reaction using Pd/MSC-30 (2623 h−1). This trend was found to reverse at 298 K (453 h−1 versus 750 h−1 for Ag0.1Pd0.9/rGO and Pd/MSC-30, respectively).

The most efficient catalysts for ORR are based on Pt and Pt alloys. However, they suffer from crossover and poisoning effects, which, associated with the Pt scarcity and high price, drives the need for alternative catalysts. Yin et al. investigated the catalytic performance of metal clusters (Au, Pd, Pt) supported on rGO sheets for the ORR [20]. The AuNCs of about 1.9 nm in size, supported on rGO (AuNC/rGO) (Fig. 9 I, II), demonstrated excellent electrocatalytic activity towards ORR. The cyclic voltammograms of O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH solutions showed an onset potential of −0.08 V (vs. Ag /AgCl) and a reduction peak of −0.19 V (vs Ag /AgCl). These results compared well with the commercial Pt/C catalyst with additional superior methanol tolerance and electrocatalytic stability. In addition, as shown in Fig. 9 III, the ORR onset potential of AuNC/rGO is more positive than that of AuNP/rGO hybrids, as well as bare rGO and AuNCs, with the higher current density observed for the hybrids with smaller AuNCs. The excellent catalytic activity of the AuNCs/rGO hybrids towards ORR was attributed to the following factors: (i) AuNCs of few atoms with low coordination number of the Au surface atoms and reduced electrophilicity lowered the activation energy of the chemisorbed O2 dissociation, favoring the O2 reduction; (ii) AuNCs improved the interfacial charge transfer during the ORR reactions; (iii) the rGO and AuNCs synergetic effects enhanced the charge transfer from Au atoms to the substrate and destabilize the intermediate species formed during the reaction; and (iv) covalent hybridization between AuNCs and rGO prevented AuNCs migration and consequent aggregation and sintering.

Later on, Wang et al. prepared p-mercaptobenzoic acid-functionalized AuNCs, Au102(p-MBA)44, deposited in porous carbon nanosheets [212]. The best hybrids (Au loading of 30 wt%) showed electrocatalytic activity towards ORR comparable with the commercial Pt/C catalysts in alkaline media. Fujigaya et al. prepared small AuNCs deposited on stacked graphene (<10 layers) coated with poly[2,2′-(2,6-pyridine)-5,5′-bibenzimidazole] and also found similar electrocatalytic activity compared to commercial Pt/C catalysts [197]. For multilayer Au25 clusters (up to 15) on rGO, Kwak et al. [186] reported a steep increase in the ORR rate with the number of Au25 layers, in contrast to what was observed for the [Ru(NH3)6]3+ complex reduction, used as control. This increase was justified by the presence of porous channels between layers that originated electrified surface-confined spaces where O2 could penetrate, thus increasing the residence time of the gas molecules and enhancing the electrocatalytic activity. The ORR followed a four-electron reduction of oxygen with a higher onset potential of ca. 0.1 V. The rGO improved the electron transfer between GCE and Au25, leading to a decrease of the difference between the oxidation and reduction potentials from 127 mV to 60 mV, and an increase of the oxidation peak from 4.5 mA to 13.4 mA.

More recently, Song et al. developed a AuNCs/rGO hybrid with high catalytic activity for both the oxygen and the 4-nitrophenol (4-NP) reduction reactions in aqueous medium [198]. The cyclic voltammograms for the catalytic activity of the AuNCs/rGO towards ORR in O2 saturated 0.1 M KOH solution yielded a cathodic reduction peak around −0.29 V, an onset potential of −0.15 V and a cathodic current density of −0.69 mA cm−2, comparable with those of commercial Pt/C catalyst.

Other types of MNCs besides AuNCs were tested for ORR [59,69,213]. A well-defined Pd5 cluster supported in MWCNTs catalyzed efficiently the reduction of O2 [69]. This catalyst with lower mass loading of Pd (2% vs. 5 wt%) than the Pd/C catalyst showed better durability, and improved mass and specific activities by 5.70 and 4.53 times, respectively. AgNCs dispersed on highly porous N-G were also found to be very effective as catalysts of ORR [59]. Stability tests showed that the AgNCs/N-G hybrid exhibited higher methanol tolerance and excellent durability with negligible effects after 8000 cycles when compared to Pt/C in alkaline solution.
A summary of the ORR electrocatalysts performances and experimental conditions for the reported MNC/CNM hybrids is provided on Table S2.

The reduction of nitro to amino groups by selective hydrogenation is a process that can be efficiently catalyzed by MNCs (Fig. 10 ). Indeed, GSH-protected AuNCs were anchored in rGO to produce a GSH-AuNCs/rGO hybrid (Fig. 10 Ia). A pyrene-terminated poly(dimethylaminoethyl acrylate) (PDMAEA) polymer was attached to the hybrid by π-π stacking interactions between the pyrene groups of the polymer and graphene. The catalytic system with temperature-tunable catalytic activity showed up to 20.8 times higher catalytic activity in 4-NP reduction by NaBH4 to 4-aminophenol (4-AP) than AuNPs/G [198]. The thermo-responsive PDMAEA polymer was added to minimize cluster aggregation and turn the reaction temperature dependent. The catalytic activity showed a noticeable turning point at 33 °C, near the lower critical solution temperature (LCST) of the polymer. For temperatures below the LCST, the polymer chains were stretched and the accessibility of 4-NP to the AuNCs was low, while for temperatures above the LCST the polymer chains collapsed increasing the exposure of the AuNCs to 4-NP. The kinetics of the reaction were monitored by UV–vis absorption of the reaction mixture at room temperature. The absorbance of the 4-NP with a maximum at 401 nm was reduced while the absorbance of the 4-AP at 301 nm increased with reaction time (Fig. 10 Ib). To improve the recyclability a fixed-bed system was constructed and the influence of the flow rate of the reaction mixture (4-NP + NaBH4) over the catalyst on the reaction kinetics was studied.

Koklioti et al. reported that the catalytic activity towards the model reduction of 4-NP to 4-AP was much higher for AuNCs (2 nm) than for AgNCs (2.5 nm) supported on functionalized graphene, both in the dark and under visible light illumination [176]. The catalytic mechanism of the reduction of 4-NP in the presence of NaBH4 by the AuNCs, in the dark, occurred via formation of an Au–H intermediate from the reaction of Au+ in the nanocluster surface with the hydride (H−) from NaBH4 (Fig. 10 II a). After this step, the reaction proceeded by the attack of the Au–H intermediate on the nitro species of 4-NP to produce the 4-AP (Fig. 10 II b). Under visible light, the reaction was accelerated due to the increase of the catalytic sites on the AuNCs surface induced by absorption of light. Additionally, the photogenerated electrons can induce the reduction of H2O with formation of H2 (Fig. 10 II c).

Although well recognized, the catalytic activity of AuNCs in liquid media is hindered by the aggregation of the nanoclusters, which seriously limits their wide application in industrial reactors. Liu et al. used AuNCs/rGO hybrids to design a high-performance microreactor system for continuous flow catalysis [189]. The AuNCs were stabilized by surface ligands that provided good dispersibility of the hybrid in aqueous medium. The nanohybrid was immobilized onto polytetrafluoroethylene membranes and tested for the hydrogenation reaction of 4-NP. A complete conversion of 4-NP was achieved with a single pass through the membrane. This system allowed both the catalytic and recovery processes to occur within a single unit, without the need for additional catalyst separation steps. A reduction of the catalytic efficiency along consecutive catalytic cycles in harsh flow conditions was studied. This type of flow-through membrane microreactor opened interesting possibilities for industrial applications.

Besides the largely studied use of MNC/CNM hybrids as catalysts in oxidation and reduction reactions, other types of reactions were also targeted. The A3 (aldehyde-alkyne-amine) coupling reaction, hydrogen production, solar energy conversion and nanoenzyme reactions are some of the examples of the broad application of such materials in catalysis.

Frindy et al. used CuNCs/G to catalyze the A3 coupling of aromatic aldehydes with five and six ring-member secondary amines and terminal alkynes [214]. For the model reaction between phenylacetylene, benzaldehyde and pyrrolidine in toluene, the CuNCs/G catalyst (1.6 wt% Cu loading and average sizes of 1–16 nm) was more efficient than other (more expensive) catalysts, such as CuNPs supported on carbon materials or metal oxides.

Hydrogen can be generated from storage compounds like Hydrazine (N2H4), which is liquid at 219–392 K and decomposes into H2 and N2 at higher temperatures. Du et al. prepared ultrafine monodisperse bimetallic Ni–Pt NCs (1.6 ± 0.2 nm) supported on graphene for H2 production [215]. The best catalyst tested (Ni84Pt16/Gr) exhibited H2 selectivity of 100% and high catalytic activity (TOF values of 133 h−1 at 298 K, and 415 h−1 at 323 K) for H2 generation from alkaline solution of hydrazine. Zeng et al. used GSH-AuNCs/Cdots hybrids integrated on spatially hierarchically ordered nanoporous TiO2 nanotube arrays (TNA) as a semiconductor platform for solar-powered photoelectrochemical water splitting [153]. The GSH-AuNCs triggered a cascading interfacial charge transfer from Cdots to TNA, which enhanced the water splitting performance under artificial solar light irradiation.
It is relevant to note that often the characterization of the metal catalysts was not clear about which material (MNCs, MNPs or even metal films) was used as catalysts.

The large specific surface area of GO and the presence of different functional groups at its surface (e.g. carboxylic, hydroxyl and epoxy groups) provide many opportunities for both covalent and non-covalent functionalization, and this has been conveniently explored in biomedical applications, such as targeting, fluorescence labelling and drug delivery.

Several studies have reported on AuNCs/GO hybrids for drug delivery. HSA- and BSA-protected AgNCs/GO hybrids were loaded with Imatinib, a first-generation tyrosine kinase inhibitor that can inhibit the growth of cancer cells [173]. The reduction in viability of K562 cells, a human erythroleukemic cell line, by Imatinib immobilized on protein-protected AgNCs/GO was found to be only 8–12% larger than Imatinib alone. Nevertheless, this hybrid could be employed as a contrasting agent in X-ray computer tomography, applied in mice models [173]. The use of GO as a multifunctional platform was further explored by loading 5-fluorouracil as a drug, hyaluronic acid as a targeting agent and GSH-AuNCs as a fluorescent label [216]. The PL emission of AuNCs and singlet oxygen generation was inhibited by energy transfer from AuNCs to GO and restored by the lysosomal hyaluronidase activity in tumor cells. Furthermore, the enzymatic degradation of hyaluronic acid and the light-induced heat effect of GO under laser irradiation stimulated the release of 5-fluorouracil from the hybrid. Overall, such platform represented a turn-on fluorescent sensor for cancer cells and an enzyme- and light-controlled agent for simultaneous chemo, photothermal and photodynamic therapy, greatly mitigating the toxic effects of materials to normal cells.

Bacterial resistance towards antibiotics is a widespread threat that can be addressed with the development of multi-mechanism antibacterial agents. With GO [217] and MNCs [218] both exhibiting antibacterial properties, it was natural to investigate the synergistic combination of the two nanomaterials. This approach is well illustrated by the assembly of paramagnetic holmium ions and AuNCs onto GO nanosheets, where the magnetically responsive Ho-GO nanosheets were vertically aligned under weak magnetic fields (< 0.5 T), providing high-density sharp edges to pierce the bacteria membrane (Fig. 11 ) [195]. Then, the AuNCs were delivered into the bacteria, inducing high oxidative stress that strongly disturbed the metabolism of both Gram-positive and Gram-negative types. Fig. 11 II shows the fluorescence imaging obtained for S. aureus after treatment with the hybrid and bare Ho-GO, with and without magnetic prealignment. The hybrid with synergistic dual antimicrobial mechanisms achieved better antimicrobial activity than bare Ho-GO, with optimized results when there was magnetic prealigment. Additionally, the hybrid showed low IC50 values, 9.8 μg.mL−1 on the basis of GO and 0.39 μM on the basis of AuNCs, indicating a high biosafety of the nanohybrid.

In addition, graphene-supported AuNCs were also shown to improve the antibacterial activity of TiO2 nanoparticles under sunlight conditions [193]. A better efficiency for solar energy utilization was achieved by overcoming the small visible light absorption of TiO2 nanoparticles. The excitation of AuNCs and TiO2 lead to the accumulation of electrons in graphene and holes in AuNCs that reacted with O2 and water to form superoxide radical anions (O2 −.) and hydroxyl radicals (OH·), respectively. An enhanced ROS production by the hybrid was reported when compared to TiO2 nanoparticles.

A final example is the development of nanothermometers. GSH-AuNCs/Cdots [116,130], or more intricately GSH-AuNCs at the surface of Cdots-doped hollow TiO2 microspheres [22], were proposed as nanothermometers. The ratiometric temperature-sensing mechanism was based on the quenching of the AuNCs PL emission with increasing temperature, while the intensity of the blue emission of the Cdots remained unchanged. The GSH-AuNCs/Cdots hybrids were applied in cellular imaging, operating either as a colorimetric temperature sensor [22,116,130] or as versatile nanothermometry device acting as “non-contact” tool for the accurate measurement of temperature gradients in living cells [116,130].

Nanohybrids built from metal nanoclusters and carbon nanomaterials of 0, 1 or 2 dimensions are a fascinating class of advanced nanomaterials that have been the subject of intensive research over the last two decades. In this review, we presented a comprehensive and integrated analysis of the advances that have taken place in this area, with respect to the synthesis, characterization, and applications of these hybrids. We covered the main synthetic paths, subdividing them into the mixing of dispersions involving non-covalent or covalent interactions between the two building blocks, in situ formation of one nanomaterial with the aid of the other, and in silico simulations. Generally, the choice of experimental synthetic pathway is dependent on the nanocarbon dimensionality and on the application intended. While for the 0D forms (Cdots) all experimental paths have been broadly employed, 1D and 2D nanocarbon supports have been combined mostly with MNCs prepared a priori. Moreover, in situ “one pot” synthetic procedures have become rather favored in recent years, following a trend to pursue simpler and more sustainable alternatives to the conventional paths.

The applications of the hybrids have explored mainly the synergistic effects emerging from having MNCs with remarkable optical and catalytic properties on one side, and CNMs with versatile and extraordinary optical, mechanical, and electrical properties on the other side. Until now, those applications have predominantly concentrated on sensing, imaging, and catalysis, corresponding to over 75% of the published work. However, novel uses have also been proposed, namely in drug delivery, antimicrobial and antibacterial activity, and as nanothermometers.

Significantly, while most of the hybrids used in optical sensing and imaging are of the MNC/Cdot type (~ 60%), carbon nanomaterials of 1D and 2D are preferred in electrochemical sensing and catalytic applications. This fact suggests that the choice of the CNM plays a pivotal role on the desired application. Future research on hybrids of MNCs and CNMs is expected to contemplate more hybrids with tunable properties, achieved through the fine control of the synthetic parameters. Further, we anticipate that advanced applications in the fields of multiple sensing and theragnostics will be the next step in the use of MNC/CNM hybrids. For this to happen on firm ground, we believe that a deeper fundamental understanding of the mechanisms underpinning the optical and electrochemical effects resulting from the interaction between the two materials is needed. Charge/electron and energy transfer are commonly invoked to explain these effects, yet with limited evidence in many cases. Furthermore, the selectivity towards specific analytes is usually not fully disclosed. In our view, the fundamental understanding of the physical and chemical properties of the hybrids, as well as of the structural factors controlling their interaction with other molecules, must move forward hand in hand with the development of advanced applications.
All the authors contributed equally.
